content.text: What It Is

Fine-tuning is the process of taking a large pretrained language model — one that has already learned general representations from billions of tokens of text — and continuing its training on a smaller, task-specific or domain-specific dataset. During pretraining, the model develops broad capabilities: grammar, factual recall, reasoning patterns, and code generation. Fine-tuning steers those capabilities toward a particular use case by adjusting the model weights on a curated dataset of examples. The result is a model that retains its pretrained world knowledge while becoming meaningfully more accurate, efficient, or stylistically aligned for a target task.

Fine-tuning sits conceptually between pure prompting and full pretraining. Prompting leaves model weights unchanged and guides behavior through carefully crafted inputs; full pretraining learns representations from scratch on massive corpora. Fine-tuning occupies the middle ground: the pretrained weights provide a strong initialization, and gradient updates on the task data refine them. The key insight is that the pretrained model has already solved most of the hard representation-learning problem — the fine-tuning stage only needs to adapt the final learned mapping. This is why fine-tuning achieves strong task performance with orders of magnitude fewer examples and compute than pretraining would require.

Modern fine-tuning has evolved far beyond simply updating all model weights. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods — LoRA, QLoRA, adapter layers, prefix tuning, prompt tuning — allow fine-tuning very large models (7B to 70B parameters) on consumer hardware by keeping most weights frozen and updating only a small, targeted subset. This makes fine-tuning accessible at a fraction of the original training cost.
Mental Model

Think of a pretrained LLM as a highly educated generalist — someone who has read enormous amounts of text and can discuss almost any topic fluently, but who has never been formally trained for your specific job. Fine-tuning is like sending that generalist through a focused apprenticeship: a few weeks of hands-on practice with real examples from your domain. They do not forget their general education; they simply become much better at the specific tasks you care about.

LoRA specifically is like giving the specialist a small notebook of adjustments rather than rewriting their entire knowledge base. The pretrained weights W represent everything they learned during training. The LoRA matrices A and B are the notebook: compact, targeted modifications that change how the model responds to your domain without touching the expensive, frozen foundation. At inference time, you add the notebook's contributions to the base weights — the generalist now performs like a specialist, and the core knowledge is fully preserved.
Why It Matters

Pretraining a frontier LLM costs millions of dollars and weeks of compute across thousands of GPUs. For most organizations and tasks, training from scratch is not viable. Fine-tuning unlocks the capability of pretrained models for specific use cases at a tiny fraction of that cost. A fine-tuned 7B model can outperform a general-purpose 70B model on a specific task, while running on hardware that costs under a hundred dollars a month.

Beyond economics, fine-tuning produces qualitatively different results from prompting alone. Prompts are limited by context length, can be inconsistent across runs, and cannot change the model's internal probability distributions. Fine-tuning adjusts the weights directly: the model learns which outputs are preferred for your task in a way that persists across all inputs without requiring per-call engineering. For production systems where consistency, latency, and cost matter, a fine-tuned smaller model often beats a prompted larger model on all three dimensions simultaneously.
Benefits

Task accuracy: Fine-tuned models routinely achieve 10 to 30 percent higher accuracy on specific tasks compared to prompting the same base model. The model learns the exact output format and reasoning style required, not just a general approximation.

Inference efficiency: A fine-tuned 7B model can outperform a prompted 70B model on narrow tasks at roughly 10x lower inference cost. Smaller fine-tuned models enable serving at scale within tight compute budgets.

Reduced prompt engineering: After fine-tuning, simple prompts suffice because the behavior is encoded in the weights. Long system prompts, few-shot examples, and careful instruction crafting all decrease in importance.

Domain vocabulary and style: Fine-tuning internalizes domain-specific terminology, naming conventions, and stylistic norms. Legal, medical, and code-generation tasks all benefit from models trained on domain-specific corpora.

Data privacy: Fine-tuning on private data on your own infrastructure avoids sending sensitive information to third-party API endpoints at inference time. The trained weights can be deployed entirely on-premise.
Consistency: Fine-tuned models produce more consistent outputs across identical or structurally similar inputs, reducing stochastic variance that makes prompted models harder to test and validate.
PEFT accessibility: LoRA and QLoRA allow fine-tuning of 7B to 65B models on a single consumer GPU, making the technique available without large infrastructure investment.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Catastrophic forgetting: Full fine-tuning can overwrite pretrained knowledge if the learning rate is too high or the training dataset is too narrow. The model may improve on the target task while regressing on general capabilities. PEFT methods mitigate this by keeping most weights frozen.

Data requirements: Fine-tuning on fewer than a hundred examples often overfits badly. Assembling a high-quality dataset of even a few thousand examples requires significant curation effort and often domain expertise.

GPU memory for full fine-tuning: Updating all weights of a 7B model in float32 requires model parameters (about 28 GB), optimizer states (about 56 GB for AdamW), and gradients (about 28 GB) simultaneously — over 112 GB total. Mixed precision and gradient checkpointing reduce this, but it remains expensive.

Hyperparameter sensitivity: Fine-tuning requires careful selection of learning rate, warmup schedule, batch size, and epoch count. Too many epochs on a small dataset leads to overfitting; too few leads to underfitting. The optimal range is narrower than during pretraining.

LoRA rank selection: Choosing rank r involves a trade-off between expressiveness and parameter count. Too low a rank may not capture the necessary task-specific structure; too high a rank wastes memory and may overfit.

Deployment complexity: Fine-tuned models require managing adapter weights alongside base model weights. PEFT adapters must be merged or loaded at inference time, adding operational complexity compared to using a hosted API.
How It Works
Full Fine-Tuning

In full fine-tuning, every parameter in the model is updated via gradient descent on the task-specific loss. For a language model, this is typically the standard cross-entropy loss over the target tokens: the model predicts the next token at each position and gradients flow back through all layers. The learning rate must be much smaller than pretraining — typically 1e-5 to 5e-5 — to avoid overwriting pretrained representations too aggressively. A warmup period (often 3 to 10 percent of total steps) followed by cosine decay is the standard schedule. Gradient clipping at 1.0 prevents unstable update steps.
LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation

LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) is based on the observation that the change in model weights during fine-tuning has low intrinsic rank. Rather than updating the full weight matrix W directly, LoRA freezes W and injects two small trainable matrices into each targeted attention projection: A of shape r by k and B of shape d by r, where rank r is much smaller than both d and k. The effective weight update is the product BA (shape d by k), which is constrained to have rank at most r. A scaling coefficient alpha/r controls the magnitude of the update. B is initialized to zero so that at the start of training, BA equals zero and the model behaves identically to the frozen base.

attrs.latex: W' = W + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A \quad \text{where} \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r},\; A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k},\; r \ll \min(d, k)

content.text: Only A and B are updated during training. W is never modified. At inference time, you can either keep the adapter separate or merge the weights by computing W_merged = W + (alpha/r)BA — a single matrix — and deploying that, which eliminates any runtime overhead compared to the original model.

The parameter count reduction is substantial. For a weight matrix with d = k = 4096 and rank r = 16: full fine-tuning trains 4096 x 4096 = 16.8M parameters per matrix. LoRA trains only r x k plus d x r = 131,072 parameters — a 128x reduction. For a 7B model with 32 transformer layers containing 4 attention projections each, LoRA with r=16 adds roughly 17M trainable parameters out of 7 billion — about 0.24 percent of the total.
QLoRA: Quantized LoRA

QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) extends LoRA by quantizing the frozen base model weights to 4-bit using the NF4 (Normal Float 4) data type, while keeping the LoRA adapter matrices A and B in bfloat16. NF4 is information-theoretically optimal for normally distributed weights, which pretrained LLM weights approximately are. A double-quantization step additionally quantizes the quantization constants, saving a further 0.37 bits per parameter. The result: a 65B parameter model requires roughly 33 GB of GPU memory instead of over 130 GB in float32, fitting on a single 48 GB GPU. Gradient computation for the LoRA adapters is performed by dequantizing the 4-bit weights to bfloat16 on the fly during the backward pass.
Other PEFT Methods

Adapter layers: small bottleneck feed-forward modules inserted between transformer sublayers. Projects down to a bottleneck dimension, applies a nonlinearity, projects back up. Only adapter weights are trained. Cannot be merged into base weights, so they add latency at inference.

Prefix tuning: prepends trainable continuous vectors to the keys and values of every attention layer. The prefix acts as soft, learnable context. The original tokens and all model weights remain unchanged. Effective for generation tasks but sensitive to prefix length and initialization.

Prompt tuning: adds learnable tokens only to the input embedding layer, not to every attention layer. Simpler and fewer parameters than prefix tuning, but less expressive. Significantly underperforms full fine-tuning at model scales below 10B.

(IA)^3: scales key, value, and feed-forward activations by learned vectors. Extremely parameter-efficient with only O(d_model) parameters per layer, but lower performance ceiling than LoRA for most tasks.
Training Schedule and Hyperparameters
Learning rate: 1e-5 to 5e-5 for full fine-tuning; 1e-4 to 3e-4 for LoRA adapters. LoRA adapters tolerate higher LR because they start from zero and the base weights are frozen.
Warmup: 3 to 10 percent of total steps with linear warmup from zero to peak LR. Avoids large gradient updates early when adapter matrices have high random magnitude.
Cosine decay: after warmup, decay following a cosine curve to near zero by the end of training. Outperforms linear decay on most benchmarks.
Gradient clipping: clip gradient norm at 1.0. Essential for stability on small datasets. For QLoRA, consider clipping at 0.3 to avoid NaN gradients with 4-bit quantization.
Gradient accumulation: accumulate gradients over N steps before updating to simulate larger effective batch sizes. For per-GPU batch size 4 and accumulation steps 8, effective batch size is 32.
Gradient checkpointing: recompute activations during the backward pass rather than storing them, trading 30 percent extra compute for significant memory savings.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the complete fine-tuning pipeline from pretrained base model through task dataset to fine-tuned output, with a detailed view of how LoRA decomposes the weight update into two low-rank matrices A and B. The key insight is that W is never touched — only the small adapter matrices carry the task-specific knowledge.

attrs.alt: Fine-tuning pipeline: pretrained LLM feeds into task dataset and fine-tuning loop to produce fine-tuned model. LoRA detail shows frozen W plus trainable BA decomposition with parameter count comparison.

content.text: In a typical LoRA setup for a decoder-only model like LLaMA or Mistral, adapters are applied to the Q and V projections (and sometimes K and O) of every transformer attention layer. Feed-forward sublayer weights are typically left frozen. At inference, the adapter can be merged: W_merged = W + (alpha/r)BA is computed once and stored, eliminating any runtime overhead compared to the original model.
When to Use It
Fine-tuning is the right choice when

You have a consistent, well-defined task with clear input-output examples and prompting alone cannot reach your required accuracy. Classification, structured extraction, and domain-specific generation are classic fine-tuning use cases.
Your task requires a specific output format that is tedious to enforce reliably via prompts — JSON schemas, code with particular style conventions, or domain-specific markup languages.
You have strong data privacy requirements and cannot send data to a hosted API. Fine-tuning on-premise puts both the data and the model under your control.

Your deployment serves high traffic at low latency and cost. A fine-tuned 7B model at 100 tokens per second on a single GPU often beats a hosted 70B API call at 5x lower latency and 10x lower cost per request.
You have assembled at least a few hundred high-quality labeled examples and expect to generate more over time as you collect production data.
Avoid fine-tuning when
You have fewer than 50 to 100 examples. The model will almost certainly overfit. Prompting with few-shot examples is more effective with tiny datasets.
Your task changes frequently. Each change requires re-curating data and re-training. If the task evolves weekly, a prompted model with updated instructions is far more maintainable.
Your examples are low quality or inconsistently labeled. Noisy data actively harms fine-tuned models — the model learns the noise with high fidelity.
You need the model to generalize broadly across many diverse topics. Fine-tuning on a narrow dataset reduces the model general capability breadth in proportion to how aggressively it is tuned.
How to Implement

The standard fine-tuning stack in 2024 to 2025 uses HuggingFace Transformers for model loading, PEFT for LoRA adapter management, bitsandbytes for quantization, and TRL's SFTTrainer for the training loop. The steps are: (1) load the base model in the target precision, (2) configure and attach LoRA adapters, (3) prepare the dataset in the instruction format, (4) configure the Trainer with hyperparameters, (5) run training, (6) save and optionally merge the adapters.
pip install transformers peft bitsandbytes trl datasets accelerate

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from trl import SFTTrainer
from datasets import Dataset
import torch

# Step 1: Load base model and tokenizer
model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token  # LLaMA has no pad token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)

# Step 2: Configure LoRA
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,               # alpha = 2 * r is a common starting heuristic
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
)

# Step 3: Attach adapters and inspect
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 4,194,304 || all params: 6,742,609,920 || trainable%: 0.0622

# Step 4: Format dataset as instruction-response pairs
def format_example(example):
    return (
        f"### Instruction:\n{example['instruction']}\n\n"
        f"### Response:\n{example['output']}"
    )

raw_data = [
    {"instruction": "Summarize this in one sentence.",
     "output": "Machine learning enables computers to learn from data."},
    # ... add hundreds or thousands of examples
]
dataset = Dataset.from_list(raw_data).map(lambda x: {"text": format_example(x)})

# Step 5: Configure training hyperparameters
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./lora-finetuned",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective batch = 32
    warmup_ratio=0.05,
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_steps=200,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    max_grad_norm=1.0,
)

# Step 6: Train
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    max_seq_length=2048,
)
trainer.train()

# Step 7: Save adapter weights
model.save_pretrained("./lora-adapter")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./lora-adapter")
Examples
Example 1: QLoRA — Fine-Tuning a 13B Model on a Single GPU

# QLoRA: 4-bit quantization + LoRA — 13B fine-tuning on 24 GB GPU
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer,
    BitsAndBytesConfig, TrainingArguments
)
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
from trl import SFTTrainer
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch

# 4-bit NF4 quantization config
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,   # quantize the quant constants too
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",        # optimal for normally-distributed weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

# Load 13B model in 4-bit NF4
model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-2-13b-hf"
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id, quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto"
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

# Prepare for k-bit training: enable gradient checkpointing + dtype casting
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)

# Include MLP layers for QLoRA (often improves performance)
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=64,
    lora_alpha=16,
    target_modules=[
        "q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",
        "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"
    ],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)

# Load public Alpaca instruction dataset (52K examples)
dataset = load_dataset("tatsu-lab/alpaca", split="train")

def format_alpaca(example):
    if example["input"]:
        return {"text": (
            f"### Instruction:\n{example['instruction']}\n\n"
            f"### Input:\n{example['input']}\n\n"
            f"### Response:\n{example['output']}"
        )}
    return {"text": (
        f"### Instruction:\n{example['instruction']}\n\n"
        f"### Response:\n{example['output']}"
    )}

dataset = dataset.map(format_alpaca)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    max_seq_length=1024,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./qlora-13b",
        per_device_train_batch_size=1,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=16,   # effective batch 16
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
        bf16=True,
        optim="paged_adamw_32bit",        # pages optimizer state to CPU
        max_grad_norm=0.3,
        save_steps=100,
        logging_steps=10,
    ),
)
trainer.train()
Example 2: Full Fine-Tuning a Small Model

# Full fine-tuning of a smaller model — all weights updated
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments,
    Trainer, DataCollatorForLanguageModeling
)
from datasets import Dataset
import torch

model_id = "microsoft/phi-2"  # 2.7B model, fits in 24 GB with BF16
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id, trust_remote_code=True)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    trust_remote_code=True,
    device_map="auto",
)
print(f"Total parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()):,}")
# Total parameters: 2,779,683,840

def tokenize(examples):
    return tokenizer(examples["text"], truncation=True, max_length=512, padding=False)

raw = [
    {"text": "Q: What is 2+2?\nA: 4"},
    {"text": "Q: Capital of France?\nA: Paris"},
    # ... add many more quality examples
]
dataset = Dataset.from_list(raw).map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])
data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    data_collator=data_collator,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./full-finetuned",
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=16,  # effective batch 32
        num_train_epochs=5,
        learning_rate=2e-5,              # low LR for full fine-tuning
        warmup_ratio=0.06,
        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
        bf16=True,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        save_strategy="epoch",
        logging_steps=5,
        max_grad_norm=1.0,
        weight_decay=0.01,
    ),
)
trainer.train()
Example 3: Inference with a Fine-Tuned LoRA Adapter

# Two inference modes: separate adapter vs. merged weights
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, pipeline
from peft import PeftModel
import torch

base_model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf"
adapter_path = "./lora-adapter"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(adapter_path)

# Mode 1: Load adapter on top of base — supports hot-swapping multiple adapters
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    base_model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto"
)
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path)
model.eval()

prompt = "### Instruction:\nSummarize this in one sentence.\n\n### Response:\n"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=100, temperature=0.1, do_sample=True)
print(tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# Mode 2: Merge adapter into base weights — zero inference overhead
merged = model.merge_and_unload()          # returns plain AutoModelForCausalLM
merged.save_pretrained("./merged-model")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./merged-model")

# Use merged model like any standard HuggingFace model
pipe = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model="./merged-model",
    tokenizer="./merged-model",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)
result = pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=100, temperature=0.1)[0]["generated_text"]
print(result)
Real-World Example: Domain-Specific Code Completion

A software company building internal developer tooling wanted a code completion model that understands its proprietary codebase conventions — specific API names, internal DSL constructs, and variable naming patterns that no public model knows. The team collected 8,000 Python function pairs from their internal repository: each pair is a function signature plus docstring (the instruction) and the complete implementation (the response). They fine-tuned CodeLlama-7B using QLoRA with rank 32 and alpha 64 on a single A100 80 GB GPU over three epochs, finishing in under four hours.

Before fine-tuning, the base model consistently hallucinated internal API names and used generic patterns from the open-source world. After fine-tuning, the model correctly generates calls to internal APIs with the right parameter names and applies company-specific error handling patterns without any prompting. On a held-out evaluation set of 200 functions, the fine-tuned model produced functionally correct implementations in 71 percent of cases versus 34 percent for the prompted base model. The 33 MB adapter is served alongside the 7B base model on two A10 GPUs using vLLM, achieving 40 tokens per second latency.
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Learning rate too high for full fine-tuning

Setting the learning rate to a pretraining-style value (e.g., 3e-4) during full fine-tuning on a small dataset causes catastrophic forgetting within the first epoch. The model overwrites pretrained representations before the task-specific signal can reinforce them. Symptoms: task performance improves then collapses, or general capability benchmarks drop sharply.

# WRONG: pretraining-style LR for full fine-tuning
TrainingArguments(learning_rate=3e-4, ...)

# CORRECT: conservative LR with warmup and cosine decay
TrainingArguments(
    learning_rate=2e-5,   # much lower than pretraining
    warmup_ratio=0.06,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    ...
)
# LoRA adapters start from zero and tolerate higher LR
TrainingArguments(learning_rate=2e-4, ...)  # fine for LoRA
Mistake 2: Computing loss over padding tokens

When batching sequences of different lengths, shorter sequences are padded. If loss is computed over padding positions, the model wastes capacity learning to predict padding and gradients are diluted with noisy signal. Always use DataCollatorForLanguageModeling or SFTTrainer, which masks padding tokens from the loss by setting their labels to -100 (ignored by PyTorch's CrossEntropyLoss).

# WRONG: loss computed over padding tokens
tokenized = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, return_tensors="pt")
loss = model(**tokenized, labels=tokenized["input_ids"]).loss  # includes padding

# CORRECT: use DataCollator that sets padding labels to -100
from transformers import DataCollatorForLanguageModeling
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)
# Cross-entropy ignores label = -100 automatically

# With SFTTrainer: loss masking is handled automatically
trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, train_dataset=dataset, dataset_text_field="text", ...)
Mistake 3: Wrong LoRA target module names

Different model families name their attention projection layers differently. Specifying wrong target_modules silently skips LoRA injection and you end up with zero trainable parameters — training proceeds but nothing is actually fine-tuned. Always verify module names match your model architecture before committing to a full training run.

# Inspect your model's layer names first
for name, _ in model.named_modules():
    if any(k in name for k in ["proj", "attn", "query", "key", "value"]):
        print(name)

# Common module names by model family:
# LLaMA / Mistral:  q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj
# GPT-2:            c_attn, c_proj
# Phi-2:            q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, dense
# Falcon:           query_key_value, dense

# After attaching LoRA, always verify:
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Must show > 0 trainable params before starting training!
Best Practices
Quality over quantity: 1,000 carefully curated, consistent examples consistently outperform 10,000 noisy ones. Invest heavily in data curation, deduplication, and quality filtering before training.

Use LoRA by default: unless you need maximum performance and have abundant GPU memory, start with LoRA (r=16 to r=64). It trains faster, uses less memory, and is easier to iterate on than full fine-tuning.

Match the instruction format exactly at inference: whatever template you used to format training examples must be replicated exactly at inference time. A mismatch in whitespace or special tokens degrades performance significantly.

Evaluate task-specific metrics, not just loss: training loss reflects fit to your data format, not real-world performance. Define and track your actual evaluation metric (F1, BLEU, exact match, win rate) from the first run.
Use paged_adamw_32bit for QLoRA: this optimizer variant pages optimizer state to CPU RAM under GPU memory pressure, enabling larger batch sizes without out-of-memory errors.

Sanity check on a tiny dataset first: before a full training run, train for 10 to 20 steps on 50 examples and verify that loss decreases, trainable parameter count is as expected, and sample outputs look reasonable.
Checkpoint frequently for QLoRA: quantized training can produce NaN gradients unexpectedly. Frequent checkpoints ensure you can resume from a known-good state without restarting from scratch.
Performance and Scalability
Training throughput is dominated by the forward and backward passes, with memory as the primary constraint. GPU memory requirements at bfloat16 with gradient checkpointing for a 7B model:
Full fine-tuning on A100 80 GB: approximately 60 to 70 GB. Feasible with batch size 1 to 2 and gradient accumulation.
LoRA (BF16 base) on A10G 24 GB: approximately 16 to 20 GB. Batch size 2 to 4.
QLoRA (4-bit base) on RTX 4090 24 GB: approximately 10 to 12 GB. Batch size 4 to 8 with accumulation.
13B QLoRA on A10G 24 GB: approximately 15 to 18 GB with gradient accumulation.
70B QLoRA on two A100 40 GB: approximately 40 to 50 GB; use device_map="auto" for tensor parallelism.

Multi-GPU training uses FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) or DeepSpeed ZeRO stages for full fine-tuning. ZeRO Stage 3 shards parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across all GPUs, enabling fine-tuning of 70B models across 8 A100 GPUs. For LoRA, simpler DDP suffices since the frozen base model fits on each GPU independently. Flash Attention 2 (set via attn_implementation="flash_attention_2") provides 2 to 4x speedup for sequences longer than 2048 tokens.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Core libraries
HuggingFace Transformers: model loading, tokenization, Trainer abstraction with distributed training, logging, and checkpointing. The de-facto standard for working with pretrained LLMs.
PEFT: LoRA, prefix tuning, adapter layers, prompt tuning. Maintained by HuggingFace. get_peft_model wraps any model with adapters in a few lines.

TRL (Transformer Reinforcement Learning): SFTTrainer for supervised fine-tuning with proper padding masking. Also provides reward model training and DPO/PPO for alignment. Recommended for instruction-following fine-tuning.
bitsandbytes: 4-bit and 8-bit quantization for QLoRA. Provides BitsAndBytesConfig and paged optimizers that move optimizer state to CPU under memory pressure.
Axolotl: configuration-driven framework wrapping HuggingFace + PEFT + TRL with a YAML config. Reduces custom training code to near zero for common setups.
LLaMA-Factory: web UI and CLI for fine-tuning LLaMA family models with support for LoRA, QLoRA, full fine-tuning, and multiple dataset formats.
Managed fine-tuning services
OpenAI Fine-tuning API: fine-tune GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4o-mini on JSONL datasets via API. No GPU management required, but higher per-token cost than self-hosted and limited model choice.
Together AI, Replicate, Modal: cloud platforms for fine-tuning open-source models. Pay per GPU-hour with managed infrastructure, useful for one-off experiments.
AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, GCP Vertex AI: enterprise managed training with full infrastructure control, monitoring, compliance features, and integration with cloud storage and secrets management.

Security and Reliability
Data poisoning

Fine-tuning on untrusted or crowdsourced data introduces poisoning risks. A small number of adversarially crafted examples can reliably implant backdoors — making the model produce specific harmful outputs when a trigger phrase is present in the input, while behaving normally otherwise. Mitigate by auditing training data for unusual patterns, using differential privacy (DP-SGD) for high-risk scenarios, and evaluating fine-tuned models on adversarial inputs before deployment.
Safety alignment degradation

Fine-tuning can undo alignment training. Instruction-tuned models have safety behaviors encoded in their weights through RLHF or DPO. Fine-tuning those weights — even on benign data — can erode safety guardrails because gradient updates do not distinguish between capability-relevant and safety-relevant weight changes. Research has demonstrated that even a small number of fine-tuning steps on innocuous data can significantly weaken safety refusals. Evaluate refusal behavior on known harmful prompt sets before and after fine-tuning any safety-critical deployment.
Training instability

QLoRA with 4-bit base weights is susceptible to NaN gradients, especially at high learning rates or with long sequences. Monitor gradient norms during training. If NaNs appear, reduce the learning rate, tighten gradient clipping (from 1.0 to 0.3), or switch from NF4 to int8 quantization. Save checkpoints frequently so training can resume from a healthy state.
Key Takeaways

Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained LLM on task-specific data to adapt its weights. The pretrained world knowledge is preserved while the model becomes more capable at the target task — at a fraction of the cost of pretraining from scratch.

LoRA is the default modern choice: inject trainable low-rank matrices A (r by k) and B (d by r) into attention projections, leaving base weights W frozen. The effective update is W + (alpha/r)BA with rank r much smaller than min(d, k), reducing trainable parameters by 100x or more while preserving nearly all task performance.
QLoRA extends LoRA by quantizing the frozen base weights to 4-bit NF4, enabling fine-tuning of 65B models on a single 48 GB GPU — hardware that was impractical for this scale before 2023.

Learning rate must be far lower than pretraining: 1e-5 to 5e-5 for full fine-tuning; 1e-4 to 3e-4 for LoRA adapters. Too high a learning rate causes catastrophic forgetting. Always pair with linear warmup and cosine decay.
Data quality dominates data quantity. A few hundred carefully curated, consistent examples outperform thousands of noisy ones. Filter aggressively, deduplicate, and validate labels before training.

Always evaluate on held-out data with task-specific metrics. Training loss does not predict generalization vs. overfitting. Define your success metric before training begins and track it throughout every run.

Fine-tuning can erode safety alignment even on benign data. Evaluate refusal behavior and general capabilities before and after training, especially when fine-tuning instruction-tuned or RLHF-aligned models.
